dialogues: Let's figure out whether those two expressions really do always give the same product. Watch how I test this idea.
I see the rows. 3 rows of 5. That's 3 times 5. And 3 times 5 is 15.
Now the columns. 5 columns of 3. That's 5 times 3. And 5 times 3 is 15. Same product! Same array, same dots. I didn't add anything or take anything away. Just read it a different way.
Here's a new array. I highlighted the rows — see them going across? How many rows, and how many in each? Build the expression and find the product.
That's right. 5 times 4 equals 20. 5 rows of 4.
Now I switched to columns. Build the column expression and find the product.
Right. 4 columns of 5. 4 times 5 equals 20. Same product again.
Another array. Rows are highlighted. Build the row expression and find the product.
That's it. 7 times 3 equals 21.
Now columns. Build it.
Right. 3 times 7 equals 21. Same product again.
That's three arrays now. Every time, two expressions, same product. 15 and 15. 20 and 20. 21 and 21.
Same product every time. But here's what we need to figure out: is this a coincidence, or does this always happen? And if it always happens... why?
That's three arrays now. Every time there were two expressions, same product. 15 and 15. 20 and 20. 21 and 21. Why do you think both expressions give the same product?
Right. No dots changed. You just looked at the same dots a different way. Same dots has to mean same total. That's why the products match.
But can we prove it even more clearly? Watch this.
Here's an array showing 3 rows of 6.
3 times 6 equals 18. Now watch what happens when I turn this array.
I turned it. Look, what were rows are now columns. What were columns are now rows.
Now it's 6 rows of 3. 6 times 3 equals 18. Same product.
And here's the important part. Did any dots disappear when I turned it? Did new dots show up?
No. Every single dot is still there. I just turned the array. Same dots, same total. That's why the product can't change.
5 rows of 3. That's 5 × 3, and 5 times 3 is 15. I'm going to rotate this array. Before I do, let's make a prediction. What expression will describe the rows AFTER I turn it?
Right. 3 times 5. The 5 rows of 3 turned into 3 rows of 5. And it has the same total of 15 still. You predicted it.
8 rows of 5. 8 times 5 equals 40. If I turn this array, what will the new row expression be?
Right. 5 times 8. Rotation swaps the row and column counts.
And will the product change?
That's it. 8 times 5. Still 40. Same dots, just turned.
Two different ways to prove the same thing. Highlighting: you read the same array two ways. Rotation: you turn the array and rows become columns. Either way, same dots, same product.
This doesn't just work for these arrays. It works for every array. Any array you can build. The two expressions will always give the same product. Always.
Right. Same dots. Whether you read them by rows or columns, whether you turn the array, the dots don't change. That's why the product can't change.

You proved something important. No matter which array you check, the two expressions always give the same product. This shows us that you can multiply the factors in any order and always get the same answer. These are turn-around facts.
Mathematicians have a name for this. They call it the commutative property of multiplication. But the idea is simple. You can change the order of the factors and get the same answer.
And here's why this is more than just interesting. It's a strategy, a plan for solving problems. If you know one fact, you already know its turn-around fact without doing any extra work.

Watch how the strategy works. If I know 4 times 6 is 24, do I need to compute 6 times 4? No, I can multiply in any order. Switch the factors. 6 times 4 is also 24. I didn't compute anything. I used what I already knew.
Your turn. You know 7 times 8 is 56. What's 8 times 7? Use the strategy.
Right. 56. You didn't need to compute, you used the turn-around facts. You can multiply the factors in any order and get the same product.
Here's an array: 3 rows of 8. That's 24 dots. Now look at these four arrays. They ALL have 24 dots. But which one is the SAME array as 3 × 8, just rotated?

Right. 8 rows of 3. That's 3 rows of 8, just turned. Same dots, same product. Now look at the others: 4 × 6, 6 × 4, 2 × 12. They all have 24 dots too, but they're different shapes. You can't rotate 3 rows of 8 and get 4 rows of 6. Same product doesn't mean same array.
Given that 9 times 4 is 36, what turn-around fact do you know?
Right. 4 times 9 equals 36. You can multiply in any order and get the same product.
You proved it: when you change the order of the factors, the product stays the same. You used highlighting, rotation, and the turn-around strategy. Let's see what you know.
